Sequences.label: prop:monotone-approx-bounds
Sequences.name: Monotone Approximation of Bounds
Sequences.question: State Monotone Approximation of Bounds.
Sequences.answer: For nonempty bounded $S$: there exist monotone sequences $(x_n),(y_n)\subseteq S$ with $x_n\to\sup S$, $y_n\to\inf S$.

Sequences.label: prop:monotone-approx-bounds
Sequences.name: Monotone Approximation of Bounds
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Monotone Approximation of Bounds.
Sequences.answer: Approximate from inside using the $\varepsilon$-witness property of sup/inf, then monotonize by taking running max (resp.\ min). Squeeze Theorem closes the gap.

Sequences.label: prop:monotone-approx-bounds
Sequences.name: Monotone Approximation of Bounds
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Monotone Approximation of Bounds use?
Sequences.answer: Least Upper Bound Property of $\mathbb{R}$; $\varepsilon$-witness property of $\sup$ and $\inf$; Squeeze Theorem.

Sequences.label: thm:MCT
Sequences.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem (MCT)
Sequences.question: State the Monotone Convergence Theorem.
Sequences.answer: Every bounded monotone sequence converges.
Sequences.hint: Increasing bounded: converges to $\sup\{x_n\}$. Decreasing bounded below: converges to $\inf\{x_n\}$.

Sequences.label: thm:MCT
Sequences.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem (MCT)
Sequences.question: What does Monotone Convergence Theorem (MCT) immediately give you?
Sequences.answer: Equivalence: LUB $\Longleftrightarrow$ MCT

Sequences.label: thm:LUB-iff-MCT
Sequences.name: Equivalence: LUB $\Longleftrightarrow$ MCT
Sequences.question: State LUB $\Leftrightarrow$ MCT.
Sequences.answer: TFAE: (i) LUB property; (ii) bounded monotone sequences converge.

Sequences.label: prop:limit-polynomial-sequence
Sequences.name: Limit of a Polynomial of a Sequence
Sequences.question: If $x_n \to x$ and $p$ is a polynomial, does $p(x_n) \to p(x)$?
Sequences.answer: Yes. $x_n \to x \Rightarrow p(x_n) \to p(x)$ for any polynomial $p$.
Sequences.hint: Induction on degree using the Algebra of Limits: sum and product rules handle each term.

Sequences.label: prop:limit-polynomial-sequence
Sequences.name: Limit of a Polynomial of a Sequence
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Limit of a Polynomial of a Sequence.
Sequences.answer: Two nested inductions: one on degree, one on exponent (for the power rule). Both use only the sum, scalar, and product rules of the Algebra of Limits.

Sequences.label: prop:limit-polynomial-sequence
Sequences.name: Limit of a Polynomial of a Sequence
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Limit of a Polynomial of a Sequence use?
Sequences.answer: Algebra of Limits (sum, scalar, and product rules); convergence of constant sequences.

Sequences.label: thm:uniqueness-limits
Sequences.name: Uniqueness of Limits
Sequences.question: State the theorem: Uniqueness of Limits.
Sequences.answer:
If a sequence $(x_n)$ converges to a real number $L$ and also converges to a real
number $M$, then $L = M$.

Sequences.label: thm:uniqueness-limits
Sequences.name: Uniqueness of Limits
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Uniqueness of Limits.
Sequences.answer: Contradiction via the triangle inequality. The adversarial choice $\varepsilon = |L - M|/2$ ensures the two $\varepsilon$-balls around $L$ and $M$ are disjoint, yet both must contain $x_n$ for large $n$.

Sequences.label: thm:uniqueness-limits
Sequences.name: Uniqueness of Limits
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Uniqueness of Limits use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; triangle inequality.

Sequences.label: thm:order-limit-theorem
Sequences.name: Order Limit Theorem
Sequences.question: State the Order Limit Theorem.
Sequences.answer: If $x_n\le y_n$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to x$, $y_n\to y$, then $x\le y$.
Sequences.hint: If $x>y$, take $\varepsilon=(x-y)/2$; eventually $x_n>y_n$, contradiction.

Sequences.label: thm:order-limit-theorem
Sequences.name: Order Limit Theorem
Sequences.question: Give a counterexample or failure case for: Order Limit Theorem.
Sequences.answer: Strict inequality $x_n<y_n$ does NOT force $x<y$: $(0)\to 0$ and $(1/n)\to 0$.

Sequences.label: thm:order-limit-theorem
Sequences.name: Order Limit Theorem
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Order Limit Theorem.
Sequences.answer: Contradiction: if $x > y$, the gap $(x-y)/2$ separates the eventual ranges of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$, producing $y_n < x_n$ for large $n$.

Sequences.label: thm:order-limit-theorem
Sequences.name: Order Limit Theorem
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Order Limit Theorem use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; trichotomy of $\mathbb{R}$.

Sequences.label: thm:squeeze-theorem
Sequences.name: Squeeze Theorem
Sequences.question: State the Squeeze Theorem.
Sequences.answer: If $x_n\le y_n\le z_n$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to L$, $z_n\to L$, then $y_n\to L$.
Sequences.hint: For $\varepsilon>0$: eventually $L-\varepsilon<x_n\le y_n\le z_n<L+\varepsilon$.

Sequences.label: thm:squeeze-theorem
Sequences.name: Squeeze Theorem
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Squeeze Theorem.
Sequences.answer: Both outer sequences enter the $\varepsilon$-band around $L$ after their respective thresholds; past the maximum threshold, the sandwich forces the middle sequence into the same band.

Sequences.label: thm:squeeze-theorem
Sequences.name: Squeeze Theorem
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Squeeze Theorem use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; transitivity of $\le$ in $\mathbb{R}$.

Sequences.label: thm:algebra-of-limits
Sequences.name: Algebra of limits
Sequences.question: State the Algebra of Limits.
Sequences.answer: If $x_n\to x$ and $y_n\to y$: $x_n+y_n\to x+y$; $x_n-y_n\to x-y$; $cx_n\to cx$; $x_ny_n\to xy$; if $y\neq 0$ then $x_n/y_n\to x/y$.
Sequences.hint: Each rule follows from the $\varepsilon$-$N$ definition; products use the rectangle decomposition.

Sequences.label: thm:algebra-of-limits
Sequences.name: Algebra of limits
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Algebra of limits.
Sequences.answer: Cases 1–3 are direct $\varepsilon/2$ splits. Case 4 uses add-and-subtract ($x_n y_n - xy = x_n(y_n - y) + y(x_n - x)$) together with the boundedness of $(x_n)$. Case 5 first establishes $|y_n| > |y|/2$ to bound the denominator away from zero, then estimates $|1/y_n - 1/y|$.

Sequences.label: thm:algebra-of-limits
Sequences.name: Algebra of limits
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Algebra of limits use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; triangle inequality; Every Convergent Sequence Is Bounded.

Sequences.label: cor:limit-respects-upper-bounds
Sequences.name: Limit Respects Upper Bounds
Sequences.question: State: limits respect upper bounds.
Sequences.answer: If $x_n\le b$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to x$, then $x\le b$.
Sequences.hint: Apply OLT with $y_n=b$.

Sequences.label: cor:limit-respects-upper-bounds
Sequences.name: Limit Respects Upper Bounds
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Limit Respects Upper Bounds.
Sequences.answer: Reduce to the Order Limit Theorem by pairing $(x_n)$ with the constant sequence $(b)$.

Sequences.label: cor:limit-respects-upper-bounds
Sequences.name: Limit Respects Upper Bounds
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Limit Respects Upper Bounds use?
Sequences.answer: Order Limit Theorem; convergence of constant sequences.

Sequences.label: cor:conv-times-bounded
Sequences.name: Convergent Times Bounded Is Bounded
Sequences.question: State: convergent times bounded is bounded.
Sequences.answer: If $(x_n)\to x$ and $(y_n)$ is bounded then $(x_ny_n)$ is bounded.
Sequences.hint: $(x_n)$ is bounded (convergent $\Rightarrow$ bounded); product of bounded sequences is bounded.

Sequences.label: cor:conv-times-bounded
Sequences.name: Convergent Times Bounded Is Bounded
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Convergent Times Bounded Is Bounded.
Sequences.answer: Both sequences are bounded; their product is bounded by the product of their bounds.

Sequences.label: cor:conv-times-bounded
Sequences.name: Convergent Times Bounded Is Bounded
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Convergent Times Bounded Is Bounded use?
Sequences.answer: Every Convergent Sequence Is Bounded; multiplicativity of absolute value.

Sequences.label: cor:conv-abs-values
Sequences.name: Convergence of Absolute Values
Sequences.question: State the corollary: Convergence of Absolute Values.
Sequences.answer:
If $(x_n)$ converges, then $(|x_n|)$ converges and
\[
|x_n| \to |x|.
\]

Sequences.label: cor:conv-abs-values
Sequences.name: Convergence of Absolute Values
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Convergence of Absolute Values.
Sequences.answer: One line: the reverse triangle inequality $\bigl||a| - |b|\bigr| \le |a - b|$ directly transfers convergence of $(x_n)$ to convergence of $(|x_n|)$.

Sequences.label: cor:conv-abs-values
Sequences.name: Convergence of Absolute Values
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Convergence of Absolute Values use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; reverse triangle inequality.

Sequences.label: thm:convergent-implies-cauchy
Sequences.name: T6.1 — Every convergent sequence is Cauchy
Sequences.question: State: convergent implies Cauchy.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_n)$ converges then $(a_n)$ is Cauchy.

Sequences.label: thm:cauchy-bounded
Sequences.name: T6.2 — Cauchy sequences are bounded
Sequences.question: State: Cauchy sequences are bounded.
Sequences.answer: Every Cauchy sequence is bounded.

Sequences.label: thm:cauchy-bounded
Sequences.name: T6.2 — Cauchy sequences are bounded
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: T6.2 — Cauchy sequences are bounded.
Sequences.answer: Identical structure to the proof that convergent sequences are bounded, with the Cauchy condition (at $\varepsilon = 1$) replacing the convergence condition. Anchor the tail at $a_N$; handle the finite head by a max.

Sequences.label: thm:cauchy-bounded
Sequences.name: T6.2 — Cauchy sequences are bounded
Sequences.question: What does the proof of T6.2 — Cauchy sequences are bounded use?
Sequences.answer: Definition of Cauchy sequence; triangle inequality; finiteness of $\{a_1, …, a_{N-1}\}$.

Sequences.label: thm:bolzano-weierstrass
Sequences.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass
Sequences.question: State Bolzano–Weierstrass.
Sequences.answer: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.

Sequences.label: thm:bolzano-weierstrass
Sequences.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Bolzano--Weierstrass.
Sequences.answer: Three-step chain: every sequence has a monotone subsequence (Monotone Subsequence Theorem); a monotone subsequence of a bounded sequence is bounded and monotone; every bounded monotone sequence converges (MCT).

Sequences.label: thm:bolzano-weierstrass
Sequences.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Bolzano--Weierstrass use?
Sequences.answer: Monotone Subsequence Theorem; Boundedness Is Inherited; Monotone Convergence Theorem.

Sequences.label: thm:cauchy-subseq-limit
Sequences.name: T6.5 — Cauchy subsequence limit
Sequences.question: State: Cauchy with convergent subsequence converges.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_n)$ Cauchy and $(a_{n_k})\to L$ then $a_n\to L$.

Sequences.label: thm:algebra-cauchy
Sequences.name: T6.4 — Algebra of Cauchy sequences
Sequences.question: State the Algebra of Cauchy Sequences.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_n),(b_n)$ are Cauchy and $c\in\mathbb{R}$: $(a_n+b_n)$, $(a_n-b_n)$, $(ca_n)$, $(a_nb_n)$ are all Cauchy.

Sequences.label: thm:algebra-cauchy
Sequences.name: T6.4 — Algebra of Cauchy sequences
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: T6.4 — Algebra of Cauchy sequences.
Sequences.answer: Cases 1, 2, 5 are direct triangle-inequality arguments. Case 3 uses the add-and-subtract decomposition $a_m b_m - a_n b_n = a_m(b_m-b_n) + b_n(a_m-a_n)$ together with the boundedness of Cauchy sequences (T6.2). Case 4 uses the uniform lower bound $|b_n| \ge \delta$ to control the denominator. No limits are named anywhere: this is an intrinsic, internal argument.

Sequences.label: thm:algebra-cauchy
Sequences.name: T6.4 — Algebra of Cauchy sequences
Sequences.question: What does the proof of T6.4 — Algebra of Cauchy sequences use?
Sequences.answer: Definition of Cauchy sequence; triangle inequality; Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded (for Case 3).

Sequences.label: thm:linear-comb-cauchy
Sequences.name: T6.8 — Linear combinations of Cauchy sequences are Cauchy
Sequences.question: State: linear combinations of Cauchy sequences are Cauchy.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_n),(b_n)$ are Cauchy and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$ then $(\alpha a_n+\beta b_n)$ is Cauchy.

Sequences.label: thm:linear-comb-cauchy
Sequences.name: T6.8 — Linear combinations of Cauchy sequences are Cauchy
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: T6.8 — Linear combinations of Cauchy sequences are Cauchy.
Sequences.answer: Immediate two-step composition of the scalar and sum cases of the Algebra of Cauchy Sequences.

Sequences.label: thm:linear-comb-cauchy
Sequences.name: T6.8 — Linear combinations of Cauchy sequences are Cauchy
Sequences.question: What does the proof of T6.8 — Linear combinations of Cauchy sequences are Cauchy use?
Sequences.answer: Algebra of Cauchy Sequences (Cases 1 and 5).

Sequences.label: thm:cauchy-divergence
Sequences.name: T6.7 — Cauchy Divergence Criterion
Sequences.question: State the Cauchy Divergence Criterion.
Sequences.answer: $(a_n)$ diverges if $\exists\varepsilon_0>0$ such that $\forall N\,\exists m,n\ge N:|a_m-a_n|\ge\varepsilon_0$.
Sequences.hint: Negation of the Cauchy condition; useful to prove divergence.

Sequences.label: thm:rapidly-cauchy
Sequences.name: T6.6 — Every rapidly Cauchy sequence is Cauchy
Sequences.question: State the theorem: T6.6 — Every rapidly Cauchy sequence is Cauchy.
Sequences.answer: If $(f_k)$ is rapidly Cauchy, then $(f_k)$ is Cauchy.

Sequences.label: thm:contractive-converges
Sequences.name: T6.10 — Every contractive sequence converges
Sequences.question: Does every contractive sequence converge?
Sequences.answer: Every contractive sequence (where $|x_{n+1} - x_n| \leq c|x_n - x_{n-1}|$ for some $c < 1$) converges.
Sequences.hint: Contractive sequences are rapidly Cauchy (after rescaling); apply the rapidly Cauchy $\Rightarrow$ Cauchy $\Rightarrow$ convergent chain.

Sequences.label: cor:convergent-series-cauchy-partial-sums
Sequences.name: T6.1c — Every convergent series has Cauchy partial sums
Sequences.question: State: convergent series have Cauchy partial sums.
Sequences.answer: If $\sum a_n$ converges and $s_n=\sum_{k=1}^n a_k$, then $(s_n)$ is Cauchy.

Sequences.label: cor:closed-interval-sequential-compact
Sequences.name: Sequential compactness of closed intervals
Sequences.question: State the corollary: Sequential compactness of closed intervals.
Sequences.answer:
Every sequence in a closed bounded interval $[a,b] \subset \mathbb{R}$ has a
convergent subsequence whose limit lies in $[a,b]$.

Sequences.label: prop:bounded-not-convergent
Sequences.name: Bounded Does Not Imply Convergent
Sequences.question: Is every bounded sequence convergent?
Sequences.answer: No. Bounded does not imply convergent.
Sequences.hint: Counterexample: $(-1)^n$ is bounded by $1$ but oscillates between $\pm 1$, never settling.

Sequences.label: prop:bounded-not-convergent
Sequences.name: Bounded Does Not Imply Convergent
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Bounded Does Not Imply Convergent.
Sequences.answer: Counterexample proof. $(-1)^n$ oscillates between $1$ and $-1$ and cannot settle near any single value: any two consecutive terms are distance $2$ apart, so $\varepsilon = 1$ forces a contradiction.

Sequences.label: prop:bounded-not-convergent
Sequences.name: Bounded Does Not Imply Convergent
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Bounded Does Not Imply Convergent use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; triangle inequality; the sequence $(-1)^n$ as a standard example.

Sequences.label: thm:conv-seq-bounded
Sequences.name: Every convergent sequence is bounded
Sequences.question: State: convergent sequences are bounded.
Sequences.answer: If $(x_n)$ converges then $(x_n)$ is bounded.
Sequences.hint: Take $\varepsilon=1$; terms beyond $N$ lie within 1 of the limit; finitely many terms before $N$ are bounded.

Sequences.label: thm:conv-seq-bounded
Sequences.name: Every convergent sequence is bounded
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Every convergent sequence is bounded.
Sequences.answer: Split at the threshold $N$ from convergence with $\varepsilon = 1$: the tail is controlled by $1 + |L|$; the finitely many head terms are bounded by their own maximum. Combine via a single max.

Sequences.label: thm:conv-seq-bounded
Sequences.name: Every convergent sequence is bounded
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Every convergent sequence is bounded use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; triangle inequality for $|\cdot|$; finiteness of $\{x_1, …, x_{N-1}\}$.

Sequences.label: thm:conv-tail-property
Sequences.name: Convergence Is a Tail Property
Sequences.question: State: convergence is a tail property.
Sequences.answer: $(x_n)\to L$ iff every $k$-tail $(x_{n+k})_{n\ge 1}\to L$.
Sequences.hint: Adding or removing finitely many terms does not affect convergence or its limit.

Sequences.label: thm:conv-tail-property
Sequences.name: Convergence Is a Tail Property
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Convergence Is a Tail Property.
Sequences.answer: The forward direction re-indexes: any $N$ that works for the full sequence works for any tail. The backward direction is immediate since the full sequence is its own $0$-tail.

Sequences.label: thm:conv-tail-property
Sequences.name: Convergence Is a Tail Property
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Convergence Is a Tail Property use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; definition of $k$-tail.

Sequences.label: thm:finite-modification
Sequences.name: Finite Modification Theorem
Sequences.question: State the Finite Modification Theorem.
Sequences.answer: If $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ differ in only finitely many terms: either both converge to the same limit or neither converges.

Sequences.label: thm:finite-modification
Sequences.name: Finite Modification Theorem
Sequences.question: What does Finite Modification Theorem immediately give you?
Sequences.answer: Tails of a Convergent Sequence Converge

Sequences.label: thm:finite-modification
Sequences.name: Finite Modification Theorem
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Finite Modification Theorem.
Sequences.answer: Past the threshold $K$ the sequences agree, so any $N$ that works for $(x_n)$ works for $(y_n)$ once raised to $\max\{N_1, K\}$. Symmetry gives the other direction; uniqueness of limits ties down the common value.

Sequences.label: thm:finite-modification
Sequences.name: Finite Modification Theorem
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Finite Modification Theorem use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; Uniqueness of Limits.

Sequences.label: cor:tails-converge
Sequences.name: Tails of a Convergent Sequence Converge
Sequences.question: Do tails of a convergent sequence converge?
Sequences.answer: If $(x_n) \to L$, then every $k$-tail converges to $L$.
Sequences.hint: Direct consequence of convergence being a tail property.

Sequences.label: cor:tails-converge
Sequences.name: Tails of a Convergent Sequence Converge
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Tails of a Convergent Sequence Converge.
Sequences.answer: Immediate forward direction of the Convergence Is a Tail Property equivalence.

Sequences.label: cor:tails-converge
Sequences.name: Tails of a Convergent Sequence Converge
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Tails of a Convergent Sequence Converge use?
Sequences.answer: Convergence Is a Tail Property.

Sequences.label: prop:monotone-unbounded-diverge
Sequences.name: Monotone Unbounded Sequences Diverge to $\pm\infty$
Sequences.question: Does an unbounded monotone sequence converge?
Sequences.answer: No. Every monotone unbounded sequence diverges (to $\pm\infty$).
Sequences.hint: Contrapositive of MCT: convergent bounded. An unbounded monotone sequence surpasses every $M$.

Sequences.label: prop:monotone-unbounded-diverge
Sequences.name: Monotone Unbounded Sequences Diverge to $\pm\infty$
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Monotone Unbounded Sequences Diverge to $\pm\infty$.
Sequences.answer: Direct from definitions. Unboundedness supplies one index $N$ beyond the threshold $K$; monotonicity propagates it to the entire tail.

Sequences.label: prop:monotone-unbounded-diverge
Sequences.name: Monotone Unbounded Sequences Diverge to $\pm\infty$
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Monotone Unbounded Sequences Diverge to $\pm\infty$ use?
Sequences.answer: Definition of divergence to $\pm\infty$; definition of increasing sequence; definition of unbounded above.

Sequences.label: thm:mct
Sequences.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem
Sequences.question: State the Monotone Convergence Theorem (MCT).
Sequences.answer: Every bounded monotone sequence converges: an increasing bounded sequence converges to $\sup\{x_n\}$; decreasing bounded below converges to $\inf\{x_n\}$.
Sequences.hint: Let $L=\sup\{x_n\}$; given $\varepsilon$, the $\varepsilon$-characterisation gives $N$ with $x_N>L-\varepsilon$; monotonicity propagates.

Sequences.label: thm:mct
Sequences.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem
Sequences.question: Give a counterexample or failure case for: Monotone Convergence Theorem.
Sequences.answer: Fails without boundedness: $(n)$ is monotone and diverges.

Sequences.label: thm:mct
Sequences.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem
Sequences.question: What does Monotone Convergence Theorem immediately give you?
Sequences.answer: Bolzano–Weierstrass

Sequences.label: thm:mct
Sequences.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Monotone Convergence Theorem.
Sequences.answer: The key pivot is the $\varepsilon$-witness property of supremum: for any $\varepsilon > 0$, some $x_N$ lies within $\varepsilon$ of $\sup S$. Monotonicity then propagates this to the entire tail.

Sequences.label: thm:mct
Sequences.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Monotone Convergence Theorem use?
Sequences.answer: Least Upper Bound Property of $\mathbb{R}$; $\varepsilon$-witness property of $\sup$; monotonicity to propagate the bound to the tail.

Sequences.label: thm:seq-determined-by-values
Sequences.name: A Sequence Is Determined by Its Values
Sequences.question: When are two sequences equal?
Sequences.answer: Two sequences $x, y : \mathbb{N} \to X$ are equal iff $x_n = y_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.
Sequences.hint: Sequences are functions; function equality is pointwise equality.

Sequences.label: thm:seq-determined-by-values
Sequences.name: A Sequence Is Determined by Its Values
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: A Sequence Is Determined by Its Values.
Sequences.answer: Direct appeal to the definition of function equality. No quantifier beyond pointwise universality is needed.

Sequences.label: thm:seq-determined-by-values
Sequences.name: A Sequence Is Determined by Its Values
Sequences.question: What does the proof of A Sequence Is Determined by Its Values use?
Sequences.answer: Definition of function equality; definition of a sequence as a function $\mathbb{N} \to X$.

Sequences.label: prop:conv-inherited
Sequences.name: Convergence Is Inherited
Sequences.question: State: convergence is inherited by subsequences.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_n)\to L$ then every subsequence $(a_{n_k})\to L$.
Sequences.hint: For $\varepsilon>0$ pick $N$; since $n_k\ge k$, $k\ge N$ forces $n_k\ge N$.

Sequences.label: prop:conv-inherited
Sequences.name: Convergence Is Inherited
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Convergence Is Inherited.
Sequences.answer: Direct restatement of Subsequences Inherit Limits.

Sequences.label: prop:conv-inherited
Sequences.name: Convergence Is Inherited
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Convergence Is Inherited use?
Sequences.answer: Subsequences Inherit Limits.

Sequences.label: prop:boundedness-inherited
Sequences.name: Boundedness Is Inherited
Sequences.question: State: boundedness is inherited.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_n)$ is bounded then every subsequence is bounded by the same bound.

Sequences.label: prop:boundedness-inherited
Sequences.name: Boundedness Is Inherited
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Boundedness Is Inherited.
Sequences.answer: Subsequence terms are a subset of the original terms; the original bound applies directly.

Sequences.label: prop:boundedness-inherited
Sequences.name: Boundedness Is Inherited
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Boundedness Is Inherited use?
Sequences.answer: Definition of bounded sequence; definition of subsequence.

Sequences.label: prop:monotonicity-inherited
Sequences.name: Monotonicity Is Inherited
Sequences.question: State: monotonicity is inherited.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_n)$ is monotone then every subsequence is monotone in the same direction.

Sequences.label: prop:monotonicity-inherited
Sequences.name: Monotonicity Is Inherited
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Monotonicity Is Inherited.
Sequences.answer: The index sequence is strictly increasing, so consecutive subsequence indices satisfy $n_k < n_{k+1}$; monotonicity of $(a_n)$ then forces $a_{n_k} \le a_{n_{k+1}}$.

Sequences.label: prop:monotonicity-inherited
Sequences.name: Monotonicity Is Inherited
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Monotonicity Is Inherited use?
Sequences.answer: Definition of monotone sequence; definition of strictly increasing index sequence.

Sequences.label: thm:finite-partition-convergence
Sequences.name: Finite Partition Convergence Principle
Sequences.question: State the Finite Partition Convergence Principle.
Sequences.answer: If $\mathbb{N}=E_1\cup\cdots\cup E_k$ (finite partition, each $E_i$ infinite) and $(a_n)_{n\in E_i}\to L$ for each $i$, then $a_n\to L$.
Sequences.hint: Take the maximum of the finitely many thresholds $N_i$.

Sequences.label: thm:finite-partition-convergence
Sequences.name: Finite Partition Convergence Principle
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Finite Partition Convergence Principle.
Sequences.answer: Take $N$ to be the maximum of the individual thresholds $N_i$. Every $n \ge N$ belongs to exactly one partition class $E_i$, which supplies the bound $|a_n - L| < \varepsilon$.

Sequences.label: thm:finite-partition-convergence
Sequences.name: Finite Partition Convergence Principle
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Finite Partition Convergence Principle use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; finiteness of the partition (guaranteeing $\max\{N_1, …, N_k\}$ is well-defined).

Sequences.label: cor:residue-class-convergence
Sequences.name: Residue-Class Convergence
Sequences.question: State Residue-Class Convergence.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ for each $r\in\{0,…,k-1\}$, then $(a_n)\to L$.
Sequences.hint: The residue classes form a finite partition of $\mathbb{N}$; apply Finite Partition Convergence.

Sequences.label: cor:residue-class-convergence
Sequences.name: Residue-Class Convergence
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Residue-Class Convergence.
Sequences.answer: Residue classes mod $k$ give a canonical finite partition of $\mathbb{N}$; apply the Finite Partition Convergence Principle directly.

Sequences.label: cor:residue-class-convergence
Sequences.name: Residue-Class Convergence
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Residue-Class Convergence use?
Sequences.answer: Finite Partition Convergence Principle; division algorithm for $\mathbb{N}$.

Sequences.label: cor:even-odd-convergence
Sequences.name: Even-Odd Convergence
Sequences.question: State Even-Odd Convergence.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_{2n})\to L$ and $(a_{2n+1})\to L$ then $(a_n)\to L$.
Sequences.hint: Special case of Residue-Class Convergence with $k=2$.

Sequences.label: cor:even-odd-convergence
Sequences.name: Even-Odd Convergence
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Even-Odd Convergence.
Sequences.answer: Special case of Residue-Class Convergence with $k = 2$.

Sequences.label: cor:even-odd-convergence
Sequences.name: Even-Odd Convergence
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Even-Odd Convergence use?
Sequences.answer: Residue-Class Convergence.

Sequences.label: thm:subseq-inherit-limits
Sequences.name: Subsequences Inherit Limits
Sequences.question: State: subsequences inherit limits.
Sequences.answer: If $a_n\to L$ then $a_{n_k}\to L$ for every subsequence $(a_{n_k})$.

Sequences.label: thm:subseq-inherit-limits
Sequences.name: Subsequences Inherit Limits
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Subsequences Inherit Limits.
Sequences.answer: The key fact is $n_k \ge k$: since the index sequence grows at least as fast as the natural numbers, any tail threshold $N$ for the full sequence is eventually passed by $(n_k)$.

Sequences.label: thm:subseq-inherit-limits
Sequences.name: Subsequences Inherit Limits
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Subsequences Inherit Limits use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; Index Growth ($n_k \ge k$ for strictly increasing index sequences).

Sequences.label: thm:conv-even-odd
Sequences.name: Convergence via Even and Odd Subsequences
Sequences.question: State convergence via even and odd subsequences.
Sequences.answer: $(a_n)\to L$ iff $(a_{2n})\to L$ and $(a_{2n+1})\to L$.

Sequences.label: thm:conv-even-odd
Sequences.name: Convergence via Even and Odd Subsequences
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Convergence via Even and Odd Subsequences.
Sequences.answer: Choose a single threshold $N$ large enough that both even and odd index subsequences are within $\varepsilon$ of $L$; then every $n \ge N$ falls into one of the two cases.

Sequences.label: thm:conv-even-odd
Sequences.name: Convergence via Even and Odd Subsequences
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Convergence via Even and Odd Subsequences use?
Sequences.answer: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; parity of natural numbers.

Sequences.label: thm:bw
Sequences.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass
Sequences.question: State Bolzano–Weierstrass.
Sequences.answer: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
Sequences.hint: Extract a monotone subsequence (Monotone Subsequence Theorem); bounded monotone sequences converge (MCT).

Sequences.label: thm:bw
Sequences.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass
Sequences.question: What does Bolzano--Weierstrass immediately give you?
Sequences.answer: Existence of Subsequential Limits, Sequential Compactness of Closed Intervals

Sequences.label: thm:monotone-subseq-theorem
Sequences.name: Monotone Subsequence Theorem
Sequences.question: State the Monotone Subsequence Theorem.
Sequences.answer: Every sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a monotone subsequence.
Sequences.hint: Peak argument: if infinitely many peak terms, they form a decreasing subsequence; if finitely many, extract an increasing one from beyond the last peak.

Sequences.label: thm:monotone-subseq-theorem
Sequences.name: Monotone Subsequence Theorem
Sequences.question: What does Monotone Subsequence Theorem immediately give you?
Sequences.answer: Bolzano–Weierstrass

Sequences.label: thm:monotone-subseq-theorem
Sequences.name: Monotone Subsequence Theorem
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Monotone Subsequence Theorem.
Sequences.answer: Peak-index dichotomy. If infinitely many peaks exist, they form a decreasing subsequence. If only finitely many exist, the failure of the peak condition beyond index $N$ allows greedy construction of an increasing subsequence.

Sequences.label: thm:monotone-subseq-theorem
Sequences.name: Monotone Subsequence Theorem
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Monotone Subsequence Theorem use?
Sequences.answer: Definition of subsequence; discreteness of $\mathbb{N}$ (to choose successively larger indices in Case 2).

Sequences.label: thm:dense-subsequence-criterion
Sequences.name: Dense Subsequence Criterion
Sequences.question: State the Dense Subsequence Criterion.
Sequences.answer: If every neighbourhood of $L$ contains infinitely many terms of $(a_n)$, then $(a_n)$ has a subsequence converging to $L$.
Sequences.hint: From $B_{1/k}(L)$ containing infinitely many terms, pick $n_k>n_{k-1}$ with $|a_{n_k}-L|<1/k$.

Sequences.label: cor:subseq-limits-exist
Sequences.name: Existence of Subsequential Limits
Sequences.question: State: every bounded sequence has a subsequential limit.
Sequences.answer: If $(a_n)$ is bounded then $\mathcal{L}(a_n)\neq\varnothing$.
Sequences.hint: By BW, extract a convergent subsequence; its limit is a subsequential limit.

Sequences.label: cor:subseq-limits-exist
Sequences.name: Existence of Subsequential Limits
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Existence of Subsequential Limits.
Sequences.answer: Immediate from Bolzano–Weierstrass: the limit of the convergent subsequence is a subsequential limit.

Sequences.label: cor:subseq-limits-exist
Sequences.name: Existence of Subsequential Limits
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Existence of Subsequential Limits use?
Sequences.answer: Bolzano–Weierstrass.

Sequences.label: cor:seq-compact-closed-intervals
Sequences.name: Sequential Compactness of Closed Intervals
Sequences.question: State sequential compactness of closed intervals.
Sequences.answer: Every sequence in $[a,b]$ has a convergent subsequence whose limit lies in $[a,b]$.
Sequences.hint: BW gives a convergent subsequence; the limit is in $[a,b]$ since $[a,b]$ is closed.

Sequences.label: cor:seq-compact-closed-intervals
Sequences.name: Sequential Compactness of Closed Intervals
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Sequential Compactness of Closed Intervals.
Sequences.answer: BW supplies the convergent subsequence; the Order Limit Theorem (via Limit Respects Upper Bounds) keeps the limit inside the closed interval.

Sequences.label: cor:seq-compact-closed-intervals
Sequences.name: Sequential Compactness of Closed Intervals
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Sequential Compactness of Closed Intervals use?
Sequences.answer: Bolzano–Weierstrass; Limit Respects Upper Bounds.

Sequences.label: thm:index-growth
Sequences.name: Index Growth
Sequences.question: State the Index Growth lemma.
Sequences.answer: If $(n_k)$ is strictly increasing in $\mathbb{N}$ then $n_k\ge k$ for all $k$.
Sequences.hint: Induction: $n_1\ge 1$; $n_{k+1}\ge n_k+1\ge k+1$.

Sequences.label: thm:index-growth
Sequences.name: Index Growth
Sequences.question: Sketch the proof of: Index Growth.
Sequences.answer: Induction. The base case is vacuous; the inductive step uses strict inequality and the discreteness of $\mathbb{N}$.

Sequences.label: thm:index-growth
Sequences.name: Index Growth
Sequences.question: What does the proof of Index Growth use?
Sequences.answer: Mathematical induction; strict monotonicity of the index sequence; the natural numbers are discrete ($m > k \Rightarrow m \ge k+1$ for $m, k \in \mathbb{N}$).
